source: Dangal (2016) — full screenplay; weighted to 'Mhari chhoriyaan chhoron se kam ke', 'gold to gold hota hai, chhora laaye ya chhori', and the final-match 'remember' beat
love_type: both — highest-grossing Indian film worldwide, AND cult resonance as a feminist rallying text in conservative heartlands
core_emotion: the fierce pride of being underestimated and then proving the doubters wrong — a daughter winning where the world bet she couldn't

dialogue_style.register: earthy rural Haryanvi — terse, unsentimental, masculine cadence that makes the pro-daughter message hit harder coming from a gruff patriarch
dialogue_style.rhythm: clipped declaratives; the father speaks in commands and verdicts, the emotion buried under hard surface
dialogue_style.stylization: grounded realism with one or two slogan-grade lines engineered to detach and travel
dialogue_style.why_it's_theirs: the Haryanvi bluntness is the point — the most progressive line in the film is spoken in the dialect of the patriarchy it overturns

signature_dialogues.line: Mhari chhoriyaan chhoron se kam ke?
signature_dialogues.construction: rhetorical question in dialect — 'are our daughters any less than sons?'; defiance compressed to a challenge
signature_dialogues.the_setup: a village that pities Mahavir for having only daughters; he flips the shame into a gauntlet
signature_dialogues.presentation: thrown back at the mocking community as a challenge, not a plea — said by the father, so it reads as authority reclaiming his daughters' worth rather than a victim asking for sympathy
signature_dialogues.payoff: reframes 'only daughters' from a curse into a boast the rest of the film proves
signature_dialogues.why_quotable: a portable feminist rallying cry usable at any 'girls can't' moment

signature_dialogues.line: Gold to gold hota hai, chhora laaye ya chhori.
signature_dialogues.construction: simple equivalence statement; brevity makes it axiomatic
signature_dialogues.the_setup: the dream of a gold medal for the country, previously assumed to require a son
signature_dialogues.presentation: stated as plain fact, no flourish — the lack of drama is what makes it land as undeniable truth
signature_dialogues.payoff: collapses the son/daughter distinction into irrelevance

signature_dialogues.line: Bas, ek baat yaad rakhna... (the pre-final-match lesson)
signature_dialogues.construction: a father's last instruction reframed: this fight isn't about a medal, it's so every girl watching believes she can
signature_dialogues.the_setup: the father is locked away and absent for the biggest match; his earlier words must carry her alone
signature_dialogues.presentation: delivered in flashback memory while she fights without him in the arena — the absence of the mentor at the peak is the masterstroke; she has to internalize the lesson, and so does the audience
signature_dialogues.payoff: her win lands as collective victory for every overlooked girl, not just personal glory
signature_dialogues.why_quotable: the 'this is bigger than you' framing is universally transferable

the_peaks.moment: Geeta's final gold-medal bout fought without her father present
the_peaks.why_loved: she wins on her own, internalizing the mentor — independence achieved at the moment of triumph

the_peaks.moment: the daughters beating the village boys for the first time
the_peaks.iconic_line: Mhari chhoriyaan chhoron se kam ke?
the_peaks.why_loved: the underestimated proving the mockers wrong in front of them

wish_fulfillment: the overlooked girl-child not just accepted but celebrated as national glory; the doubters publicly humbled
character_magnetism: Mahavir: the hard, unyielding father whose tyranny is revealed as fierce love — and Geeta's transformation from reluctant to unstoppable
audacity: spending its emotional climax on the MENTOR being absent — denying the audience the father-daughter reunion at the peak to make a point about independence
portability: 'Mhari chhoriyaan chhoron se kam ke' as a feminist slogan; the wrestling-as-empowerment image
why_cult_not_just_good: it became a text people cite in arguments about gender and aspiration — quoted as evidence, not just enjoyed
transferable_love_triggers: Put your most progressive line in the mouth (and dialect) of the very culture it challenges — the contrast multiplies its force.
Reframe a source of shame as a boast/challenge early ('are our daughters less?'), then spend the film proving the boast.
Remove the mentor at the climactic test so the protagonist must internalize the lesson alone — the absence makes independence the real victory.
State your thesis as a flat axiom ('gold is gold') with zero flourish; understatement reads as undeniable truth and travels as a slogan.
Frame the personal win as a win for everyone like the hero ('so every girl believes') to convert private triumph into collective catharsis.
confidence: high — key lines confirmed verbatim in script; box-office and cultural status well documented
